title: Noise spectroscopy of insulating and itinerant altermagnets
authors: Lucas V. Pupim, Mathias S. Scheurer
abstract: One of the central goals in the emergent field of altermagnetism is the unambiguous experimental identification and characterization of altermagnetic order across a variety of compounds. This motivates exploring tools that can clearly distinguish altermagnets from antiferromagnets, based on symmetry signatures, and offer access to the dominant orbital character (e.g., $d$-wave vs. $g$-wave) of the magnetic order parameter. In this work, we theoretically explore the potential of noise magnetometry for this task, studying contributions from both magnons and itinerant electrons in different regimes and scenarios. While altermagnetism and antiferromagnetism also lead to different noise spectra for magnons, we find the most striking and symmetry-sensitive signatures in the charge fluctuations of itinerant altermagnets. Both for the homogeneous bulk case and in the presence of strain and/or around domain walls, we identify noise contributions that are only permitted by symmetry in the altermagnet and, thus, provide a unique signature of altermagnetism. Furthermore, the angular dependence of noise around domain walls also offers access to the orbital character of the altermagnet. On a more technical note, we discuss the role and relevance of lattice effects related to the dipole tensor. We hope that our work will help pave the way towards the clear experimental identification of altermagnetism across a wide range of candidate materials.
summary: This paper proposes using noise magnetometry to experimentally identify altermagnetism. By analyzing the magnetic noise spectra of both insulating and metallic altermagnets, the authors show that charge fluctuations in itinerant systems provide the most striking signatures to distinguish altermagnets from antiferromagnets. They demonstrate that symmetry-breaking perturbations, such as strain or domain walls, induce unique, measurable changes in qubit relaxation and dephasing rates. This work provides a roadmap for using quantum sensors like NV centers to characterize the orbital symmetry of new magnetic materials.
main_problem: Developing unambiguous experimental methods to distinguish altermagnetic (AM) order from antiferromagnetic (AFM) order and identifying tools to access the orbital character of the magnetic order parameter.
main_result: Noise spectroscopy, particularly through charge fluctuations in itinerant altermagnets, provides unique symmetry-sensitive signatures (such as finite relaxation rate differences under strain) that distinguish AM from AFM. Additionally, the angular dependence of noise around domain walls can reveal the orbital character (d-wave vs. g-wave) of the order parameter.
method: The study employs Linear Spin Wave Theory (LSWT) for the insulating regime and a tight-binding model for the itinerant regime, utilizing the Holstein-Primakoff transformation, Bogoliability transformations (Colpa method), and the Kubo formula to calculate magnetic noise tensors and qubit decoherence rates.
model_or_system: The paper investigates both insulating altermagnets (modeled via a Heisenberg Hamiltonian on a checkerboard lattice) and itinerant altermagnets (modeled via an electronic tight-binding model with spin-orbit coupling). The experimental probe is modeled as a qubit (e.g., an NV center) sensitive to magnetic field fluctuations.
key_observables: Qubit relaxation rate (1/T1), dephasing rate (1/T2), magnetic noise tensor components (N_ab), relaxation rate difference (delta_Gamma), and Hall conductivity (sigma_H).
important_parameters: Zeeman field (h), temperature (T), distance between probe and sample (d), lattice spacing (a), and strain parameters (epsilon).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the continuum limit (d >> a) for certain spatial analyses, uses the 1/S expansion in the spin-wave theory, and neglects the momentum dependence of screening and non-coplanarity in domain wall textures.
figures_summary: Figure 2 compares magnon modes in AFM and AM; Figure 3 shows parallel noise components and their dependence on distance d; Figure 6 illustrates the difference in relaxation rates.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of AM/AFM distinction, then details the insulating regime using spin-wave theory, moves to the itinerant regime focusing on charge fluctuations and symmetry breaking (strain/domain walls), and concludes with technical discussions on lattice effects and the dipole tensor.
why_interesting: The paper explores how local magnetic symmetries and topological features (like Berry curvature) manifest in the noise spectrum of a quantum probe, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and the characterization of novel many-body phases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Schematic checkerboard lattice and its couplings in the insulating (Heisenberg model) and metallic (tight- binding) regime. In the insulating regime, magnonic exci- tations generate a fluctuating magnetic field B(t), while in the conducting regime, current fluctuations cause these mag- netic oscillations. The magnetic noise, in turn, is detected as shown in (b). The fluctuating magnetic field couples to the qubit and introduces noise into the coherence factor φ(t). This noise, in turn, is detected as a decoherence rate 1/T2. Alternatively, one can start the qubit in the state |1⟩and see the effect of magnetic noise in the relaxation rate 1/T1.
FIG. 2: (a) Antiferromagnetic (η = 1, i.e., J1 = 1, J2 = 0) and (b) altermagnetic (η = 1/ √
FIG. 3: Parallel noise N∥of a qubit probe aligned in-plane (θ = π/2, ϕ = 0) for the (a) antiferromagnetic (η = 1) and (b) altermagnetic (η = 1/ √

FIG. 4: Angular dependence of parallel noise N∥(ω) ∝1/T2. The qubit axis lies in the sample’s plane (θ = π/2) and we vary the azimuthal angle (ϕ). In (a) we use d = 2, while in (b) we use d = 10. For larger distances (d ≫a), we note that the noise tends to becoming isotropic, while for shorter distances (d ∼a), N∥(ω) is suppressed when the qubit is aligned with the magnetic order (ϕ = 0◦, 180◦). In (c) we show that the relative anisotropy δN∥decays as d increases, eventually showing the continuum limit, where the noise is isotropic. For all plots, we use T = 1, ω = 0.25 and h = 0.5.

FIG. 5: (a) Berry curvature for the AM. The solid and dashed lines show the corresponding Fermi surfaces. (b) Imaginary part of the Hall conductance as a function of axial strain (see text). Here, we used the Poisson ration ν = 0.25. (c) Spatial profile of the (normalized) effective potential due to a domain wall. (d) Imaginary part of the Hall conductance at different positions (angles) of the domain wall for a qubit at kF d = 100. We used a kF W = 10 and R/W = 25. In all plots, we considered µ = 0.3, δt2 = 0.3, αR = 0.02 and ωq = 10−4. Note that the relevant contribution to the decoherence difference δΓ comes from momenta q with |q| = 1/2d.
